title: --userns=keep-id no longer working for privileged containers
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

/kind bug

**Description**

The [kas](https://github.com/siemens/kas) project uses `--userns=keep-id` to get a user mapping between the user calling padman run on the host and a specific user (build) inside the container. This worked quite well in the past, but got broken with 80c0fceb24b70a85f3f2ca8be29f4a131c0881d4. We now end up in the error

```
Error: keep-id is only supported in rootless mode
```

As the mentioned commit is talking about adding support for --userns=nomap it's not clear if this change was intended. If this change was really intended: Is there a convenient way of ending up with this user mapping in both podman version worlds?

@rhatdan is the author of the mentioned commit. I hope it's quite easy for him to answer that...

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. podman 3.4.7 (Fedora 35)
```
sudo podman run -i -t --userns=keep-id debian:11
root@994d1ea087de:/#
```
2. podman 4.1.0 (Fedora 36)
```
sudo podman run -i -t --userns=keep-id debian:11
[...]
Error: keep-id is only supported in rootless mode
```

**Describe the results you received:**
```
Error: keep-id is only supported in rootless mode
```

**Describe the results you expected:**
The root user (inside the container) should be mapped to the host-user that executed podman run.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 4.1.0
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**


**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**

No (I'm quite sure it will would not help)

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
Good old bare metal, running Fedora 35/36.

comments.body: Why do use keep-id when you run as root user? Root already uses a 1:1 mapping so there is no reason for this option.

> Why do use keep-id when you run as root user? Root already uses a 1:1 mapping so there is no reason for this option.

I removed the `1:1` from the issue description, that was misleading and confused me as well.

The user calling `podman run` on the host should be mapped to the root user inside the container.

There is no way to know guaranteed who the user is running the container.

You should just do 
```
$ sudo podman run --user $(id -u):$(id -g) fedora id
uid=3267(3267) gid=3267(3267) groups=3267(3267)
```

Thanks for all the super fast support. It seems that we need `--userns=keep-id` for rootless containers only, so we should be able to deal with that.

The proposed `--user $(id -u):$(id -g)` did not work because the username inside the container would become the numerical value, which does not work together with expectations (like sudo configuration) inside the container.

The documentation about `keep-id` tells me that it should simply be ignored in containers created by the root user. Taken from https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-pod-create.1.html?highlight=keep-id#userns-mode
```
keep-id: creates a user namespace where the current rootless user’s UID:GID are mapped to the same 
values in the container. This option is ignored for containers created by the root user.
```
It's definitly no longer ignored. Might be a documentation bug now, but it's definitely an API change.
